revision.text:
{{Cite sources|date=2021st year 12月}} {{right| [[File:Hokiji03ds1536.jpg|thumb|none|Hōki-ji Pagoda (Asuka period)]] [[File:Yakushiji Toto.jpg|thumb|none|Yakushi-ji East Pagoda (Nara period)]] [[File:Ichijoji Kasai13bs4272.jpg|thumb|none|Ichijō-ji Pagoda (Heian period)]] [[File:Kofukuji03s3200.jpg|thumb|none|Kōfuku-ji Pagoda (Kamakura period)]] }}
'''Three-storied pagodas''' (三重塔, ''Sanjū-no-tō'') are a type of [[Buddhist]] pagoda used to enshrine the relics of [[Shakyamuni]]. Other similar forms include {{ill|Five-storied pagoda|8=ja|9=五重塔|lt=Five-storied pagodas}}.
== Overview == Pagodas trace their origin to [[ancient India]], where [[stupas]] were built as early as the [[3rd century BCE]] to enshrine the relics of [[Shakyamuni]]. These hemispherical stupas evolved into multi-tiered structures as they spread to [[China]] and incorporated elements of Chinese architecture, transforming into tower-like structures. From China, they were introduced to [[Korea]] and subsequently to [[Japan]]. Japan retains a significant number of wooden pagodas, unlike China and Korea, where fewer examples survive.
In Japan, three-storied pagodas are found in various Buddhist temples and Shinto shrines, often serving as landmarks. In addition to wooden pagodas, structures made of stone, tiles, or iron exist, with modern examples built from [[reinforced concrete]]. Other multi-tiered pagodas include seven, nine, or thirteen-story structures, though no wooden examples of seven or nine stories remain today. Notably, Nara's [[Tanzan Shrine]] features a unique wooden thirteen-story pagoda where the roofs are tightly stacked with little space between tiers.
Chinese pagodas often allow visitors to ascend to the upper floors, but Japanese wooden three-storied pagodas are typically not accessible beyond the first floor due to the complex wooden framework that supports the eaves. == See Also == * [[Pagoda]] * {{ill|Five-storied pagoda|8=ja|9=五重塔}} * [[Tahōtō]] * [[Eighteen Deities of Ancient Buddhist Pagodas]]
{{Buddhism-stub}}

{{Cite sources|date=2021st year 12月}} {{right| [[File:Hokiji03ds1536.jpg|thumb|none|Hōki-ji Pagoda (Asuka period)]] [[File:Yakushiji Toto.jpg|thumb|none|Yakushi-ji East Pagoda (Nara period)]] [[File:Ichijoji Kasai13bs4272.jpg|thumb|none|Ichijō-ji Pagoda (Heian period)]] [[File:Kofukuji03s3200.jpg|thumb|none|Kōfuku-ji Pagoda (Kamakura period)]] }}
'''Three-storied pagodas''' (三重塔, ''Sanjū-no-tō'') are a type of [[Buddhist]] pagoda used to enshrine the relics of [[Shakyamuni]]. Other similar forms include {{ill|Five-storied pagoda|8=ja|9=五重塔|lt=Five-storied pagodas|12=draft}}.
== Overview == Pagodas trace their origin to [[ancient India]], where [[stupas]] were built as early as the [[3rd century BCE]] to enshrine the relics of [[Shakyamuni]]. These hemispherical stupas evolved into multi-tiered structures as they spread to [[China]] and incorporated elements of Chinese architecture, transforming into tower-like structures. From China, they were introduced to [[Korea]] and subsequently to [[Japan]]. Japan retains a significant number of wooden pagodas, unlike China and Korea, where fewer examples survive.
In Japan, three-storied pagodas are found in various Buddhist temples and Shinto shrines, often serving as landmarks. In addition to wooden pagodas, structures made of stone, tiles, or iron exist, with modern examples built from [[reinforced concrete]]. Other multi-tiered pagodas include seven, nine, or thirteen-story structures, though no wooden examples of seven or nine stories remain today. Notably, Nara's [[Tanzan Shrine]] features a unique wooden thirteen-story pagoda where the roofs are tightly stacked with little space between tiers.
Chinese pagodas often allow visitors to ascend to the upper floors, but Japanese wooden three-storied pagodas are typically not accessible beyond the first floor due to the complex wooden framework that supports the eaves. == See Also == * [[Pagoda]] * {{ill|Five-storied pagoda|8=ja|9=五重塔|12=draft}} * [[Tahōtō]] * {{ill|Eighteen Deities of Ancient Buddhist Pagodas|ja|仏塔古寺十八尊}}

'''Three-storied pagodas''' (三重塔, ''Sanjū-no-tō'') are a type of [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] pagoda used to enshrine the relics of [[Shakyamuni Tathāgata]]. Other similar forms include {{ill|Five-storied pagoda|8=ja|9=五重塔|lt=Five-storied pagodas|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five-storied pagoda|qq=}}.

== Overview ==
Pagodas trace their origin to [[:en:Ancient India|Ancient India]], where [[:en:stupas|stupas]] were built as early as the [[:en:3rd century BCE|3rd century BCE]] to enshrine the relics of [[Shakyamuni Tathāgata]]. These hemispherical stupas evolved into multi-tiered structures as they spread to [[:en:China|China]] and incorporated elements of Chinese architecture, transforming into tower-like structures. From China, they were introduced to [[:en:Korea|Korea]] and subsequently to [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Japan retains a significant number of wooden pagodas, unlike China and Korea, where fewer examples survive.

In Japan, three-storied pagodas are found in various Buddhist temples and Shinto shrines, often serving as landmarks. In addition to wooden pagodas, structures made of stone, tiles, or iron exist, with modern examples built from [[:en:Reinforced concrete|Reinforced concrete]]. Other multi-tiered pagodas include seven, nine, or thirteen-story structures, though no wooden examples of seven or nine stories remain today. Notably, Nara's [[Tanzan Shrine]] features a unique wooden thirteen-story pagoda where the roofs are tightly stacked with little space between tiers.

Chinese pagodas often allow visitors to ascend to the upper floors, but Japanese wooden three-storied pagodas are typically not accessible beyond the first floor due to the complex wooden framework that supports the eaves.

'''Three-storied pagodas''' (三重塔, ''Sanjū-no-tō'') are a type of [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] pagoda used to enshrine the relics of [[Shakyamuni Tathāgata]]. Other similar forms include {{ill|Five-storied pagoda|8=ja|9=五重塔|lt=Five-storied pagodas|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five-storied pagoda|qq=}}.

== Overview ==
Pagodas trace their origin to [[:en:Ancient India|Ancient India]], where [[:en:stupas|stupas]] were built as early as the [[:en:3rd century BCE|3rd century BCE]] to enshrine the relics of [[Shakyamuni Tathāgata]]. These hemispherical stupas evolved into multi-tiered structures as they spread to [[:en:China|China]] and incorporated elements of Chinese architecture, transforming into tower-like structures. From China, they were introduced to [[:en:Korea|Korea]] and subsequently to [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Japan retains a significant number of wooden pagodas, unlike China and Korea, where fewer examples survive.

In Japan, three-storied pagodas are found in various Buddhist temples and Shinto shrines, often serving as landmarks. In addition to wooden pagodas, structures made of stone, tiles, or iron exist, with modern examples built from [[:en:Reinforced concrete|Reinforced concrete]]. Other multi-tiered pagodas include seven, nine, or thirteen-story structures, though no wooden examples of seven or nine stories remain today. Notably, Nara's [[Tanzan Shrine]] features a unique wooden thirteen-story pagoda where the roofs are tightly stacked with little space between tiers.

Chinese pagodas often allow visitors to ascend to the upper floors, but Japanese wooden three-storied pagodas are typically not accessible beyond the first floor due to the complex wooden framework that supports the eaves.

'''Three-storied pagodas''' (三重塔, ''Sanjū-no-tō'') are a type of [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] pagoda used to enshrine the relics of [[Shakyamuni Tathāgata]]. Other similar forms include {{ill|Five-storied pagoda|8=ja|9=五重塔|lt=Five-storied pagodas|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five-storied pagoda|qq=}}.

== Overview ==
Pagodas trace their origin to [[:en:Ancient India|Ancient India]], where [[:en:stupas|stupas]] were built as early as the [[:en:3rd century BCE|3rd century BCE]] to enshrine the relics of [[Shakyamuni Tathāgata]]. These hemispherical stupas evolved into multi-tiered structures as they spread to [[:en:China|China]] and incorporated elements of Chinese architecture, transforming into tower-like structures. From China, they were introduced to [[:en:Korea|Korea]] and subsequently to [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Japan retains a significant number of wooden pagodas, unlike China and Korea, where fewer examples survive.

In Japan, three-storied pagodas are found in various Buddhist temples and Shinto shrines, often serving as landmarks. In addition to wooden pagodas, structures made of stone, tiles, or iron exist, with modern examples built from [[:en:Reinforced concrete|Reinforced concrete]]. Other multi-tiered pagodas include seven, nine, or thirteen-story structures, though no wooden examples of seven or nine stories remain today. Notably, Nara's [[Tanzan Shrine]] features a unique wooden thirteen-story pagoda where the roofs are tightly stacked with little space between tiers.

Chinese pagodas often allow visitors to ascend to the upper floors, but Japanese wooden three-storied pagodas are typically not accessible beyond the first floor due to the complex wooden framework that supports the eaves.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Three-storied pagodas]][[ja:三重塔]][[zh:三重塔]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11357687]] Wikidata name: three-storied pagoda [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced|date=2021st year 12月}} {{right| [[File:Hokiji03ds1536.jpg|thumb|none|Hōki-ji Pagoda (Asuka period)]] [[File:Yakushiji Toto.jpg|thumb|none|Yakushi-ji East Pagoda (Nara period)]] [[File:Ichijoji Kasai13bs4272.jpg|thumb|none|Ichijō-ji Pagoda (Heian period)]] [[File:Kofukuji03s3200.jpg|thumb|none|Kōfuku-ji Pagoda (Kamakura period)]] }}
'''Three-storied pagodas''' (三重塔, ''Sanjū-no-tō'') are a type of [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] pagoda used to enshrine the relics of [[Shakyamuni Tathāgata]]. Other similar forms include {{ill|Five-storied pagoda|8=ja|9=五重塔|lt=Five-storied pagodas|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five-storied pagoda|qq=}}.
== Overview == Pagodas trace their origin to [[:en:Ancient India|Ancient India]], where [[:en:stupas|stupas]] were built as early as the [[:en:3rd century BCE|3rd century BCE]] to enshrine the relics of [[Shakyamuni Tathāgata]]. These hemispherical stupas evolved into multi-tiered structures as they spread to [[:en:China|China]] and incorporated elements of Chinese architecture, transforming into tower-like structures. From China, they were introduced to [[:en:Korea|Korea]] and subsequently to [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Japan retains a significant number of wooden pagodas, unlike China and Korea, where fewer examples survive.
In Japan, three-storied pagodas are found in various Buddhist temples and Shinto shrines, often serving as landmarks. In addition to wooden pagodas, structures made of stone, tiles, or iron exist, with modern examples built from [[:en:Reinforced concrete|Reinforced concrete]]. Other multi-tiered pagodas include seven, nine, or thirteen-story structures, though no wooden examples of seven or nine stories remain today. Notably, Nara's [[Tanzan Shrine]] features a unique wooden thirteen-story pagoda where the roofs are tightly stacked with little space between tiers.
Chinese pagodas often allow visitors to ascend to the upper floors, but Japanese wooden three-storied pagodas are typically not accessible beyond the first floor due to the complex wooden framework that supports the eaves. == See Also == * [[:en:Pagoda|Pagoda]] * {{ill|Five-storied pagoda|8=ja|9=五重塔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five-storied pagoda|qq=}} * [[Tahōtō]] * {{ill|Eighteen Deities of Ancient Buddhist Pagodas|ja|仏塔古寺十八尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eighteen Deities of Ancient Buddhist Pagodas|1=Eighteen Deities of Ancient Buddhist Pagodas|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9388421}}
{{Buddhism-stub}}

'''Three-storied pagodas''' (三重塔, ''Sanjū-no-tō'') are a type of [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] pagoda used to enshrine the relics of [[Shakyamuni Tathāgata]]. Other similar forms include {{ill|Five-storied pagoda|8=ja|9=五重塔|lt=Five-storied pagodas|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five-storied pagoda|qq=}}.

== Overview ==
Pagodas trace their origin to [[:en:Ancient India|Ancient India]], where [[:en:stupas|stupas]] were built as early as the [[:en:3rd century BCE|3rd century BCE]] to enshrine the relics of [[Shakyamuni Tathāgata]]. These hemispherical stupas evolved into multi-tiered structures as they spread to [[:en:China|China]] and incorporated elements of Chinese architecture, transforming into tower-like structures. From China, they were introduced to [[:en:Korea|Korea]] and subsequently to [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Japan retains a significant number of wooden pagodas, unlike China and Korea, where fewer examples survive.

In Japan, three-storied pagodas are found in various Buddhist temples and Shinto shrines, often serving as landmarks. In addition to wooden pagodas, structures made of stone, tiles, or iron exist, with modern examples built from [[:en:Reinforced concrete|Reinforced concrete]]. Other multi-tiered pagodas include seven, nine, or thirteen-story structures, though no wooden examples of seven or nine stories remain today. Notably, Nara's [[Tanzan Shrine]] features a unique wooden thirteen-story pagoda where the roofs are tightly stacked with little space between tiers.

Chinese pagodas often allow visitors to ascend to the upper floors, but Japanese wooden three-storied pagodas are typically not accessible beyond the first floor due to the complex wooden framework that supports the eaves.

Pagodas trace their origin to [[:en:Ancient India|Ancient India]], where [[:en:stupas|stupas]] were built as early as the [[:en:3rd century BCE|3rd century BCE]] to enshrine the relics of [[Shakyamuni Tathāgata]]. These hemispherical stupas evolved into multi-tiered structures as they spread to [[:en:China|China]] and incorporated elements of Chinese architecture, transforming into tower-like structures. From China, they were introduced to [[:en:Korea|Korea]] and subsequently to [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Japan retains a significant number of wooden pagodas, unlike China and Korea, where fewer examples survive.

In Japan, three-storied pagodas are found in various Buddhist temples and Shinto shrines, often serving as landmarks. In addition to wooden pagodas, structures made of stone, tiles, or iron exist, with modern examples built from [[:en:Reinforced concrete|Reinforced concrete]]. Other multi-tiered pagodas include seven, nine, or thirteen-story structures, though no wooden examples of seven or nine stories remain today. Notably, Nara's [[Tanzan Shrine]] features a unique wooden thirteen-story pagoda where the roofs are tightly stacked with little space between tiers.

Chinese pagodas often allow visitors to ascend to the upper floors, but Japanese wooden three-storied pagodas are typically not accessible beyond the first floor due to the complex wooden framework that supports the eaves.

Notably, Nara's [[Tanzan Shrine]] features a unique wooden thirteen-story pagoda where the roofs are tightly stacked with little space between tiers.

Chinese pagodas often allow visitors to ascend to the upper floors, but Japanese wooden three-storied pagodas are typically not accessible beyond the first floor due to the complex wooden framework that supports the eaves.

'''Three-storied pagodas''' (三重塔, ''Sanjū-no-tō'') are a type of {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism}} pagoda used to enshrine the relics of [[Shakyamuni Tathāgata]]. Other similar forms include {{ill|Five-storied pagoda|8=ja|9=五重塔|lt=Five-storied pagodas|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five-storied pagoda|qq=}}.

== Overview ==
Pagodas trace their origin to [[:en:Ancient India|Ancient India]], where [[:en:stupas|stupas]] were built as early as the [[:en:3rd century BCE|3rd century BCE]] to enshrine the relics of [[Shakyamuni Tathāgata]]. These hemispherical stupas evolved into multi-tiered structures as they spread to {{ill|China|en|China|WD=Q148|lt=China}} and incorporated elements of Chinese architecture, transforming into tower-like structures. From China, they were introduced to {{ill|Korea|en|Korea|WD=Q18097|lt=Korea}} and subsequently to {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. In Japan, three-storied pagodas are found in various Buddhist temples and Shinto shrines, often serving as landmarks. In addition to wooden pagodas, structures made of stone, tiles, or iron exist, with modern examples built from {{ill|Reinforced concrete|en|Reinforced concrete|WD=Q184190|lt=Reinforced concrete}}. Other multi-tiered pagodas include seven, nine, or thirteen-story structures, though no wooden examples of seven or nine stories remain today. Notably, Nara's [[Tanzan Shrine]] features a unique wooden thirteen-story pagoda where the roofs are tightly stacked with little space between tiers.

Chinese pagodas often allow visitors to ascend to the upper floors, but Japanese wooden three-storied pagodas are typically not accessible beyond the first floor due to the complex wooden framework that supports the eaves.

Notably, Nara's [[Tanzan Shrine]] features a unique wooden thirteen-story pagoda where the roofs are tightly stacked with little space between tiers.

Chinese pagodas often allow visitors to ascend to the upper floors, but Japanese wooden three-storied pagodas are typically not accessible beyond the first floor due to the complex wooden framework that supports the eaves.
